title: Lesson 299
transcript: Let's talk about a couple of ways in which we go about protecting our resources. And let's start with one of the major assets, resources that organizations are gonna have, and that is data. Now, as John talked about, it's very difficult to protect data or protect the media the data is stored on for a very long period of time. The challenge here is that sometimes we have to keep data for a very long period of time. For example, the longest retention period that I've ever come across was 150 years. Sounds crazy, right? So I was working for a pension provider and they'd done the math, they'd figured this out. They looked at someone might start working at say, 18 years old and start collecting a pension. So they have to start storing information at that point. And then that individual might work up to 60 or 70 or, I don't know, when do we work now? When do we get to retire? About a year or two before we die? So they work right up until 60, 70, 80. And so you have to maintain their pension data over all those decades. And then we're on their deathbed. They marry a 20 year old. And now that 20 year old starts collecting their pension benefits for the next 50, 60 years. And so they had done the math on this and they had determined that they needed to keep data for 150 years. How do you do that? As we've talked about, most of the media that we use out there doesn't last anywhere near that long. The MTBF, the mean time between failure for something like a hard drive is three to five years. For things like optical media, like CDs, DVDs, it's something like seven to 20 years. Paper can last a good long time, but even after 50, 100 years, especially if you leave it in a moldy basement, it's only gonna last a couple years. The point is this, none of this media is gonna last reliably for 150 years, unless you're chiseling it in stone. So if you need to keep data and protect data for a very long period of time. How do you actually do this? And the answer is, it's all about a process. You have to put processes in place to constantly move the data to new media. and not just move the data to new media, but also probably update the file format. I've got files from when I went to university and they're like word perfect. How do you even open a word perfect file now? I have no idea. So you've got to continually move the data to new media, update the file format to more recent types of files, and you also have to look at the protection or the encryption that's being applied to that data. So 20 years ago, was a 56-bit DES key good enough to protect data? Absolutely. Is a 56-bit DES key good enough nowadays? Absolutely not. So when you boil it down and you think about it, what does it take to protect data for a very long creative time? It's about having processes in place where you periodically move the data to new media, update the file format, update the encryption mechanisms, etc. And some of the considerations you should look at when you're putting in place one of these media management processes is the confidentiality that you need to attain. Is a 256-bit key gonna be good enough 30 years from now? What sort of access speeds do you need? If you're storing these tapes in a mountain somewhere and you need to access them in an hour, that's not going to work. How portable does the data need to be? How easily do you need to be able to open up these old file formats and get at the data? I've heard of cases where people that have been wanting to access old data, they've literally had to go to a museum and get their hands on an old type of magnetic reading device or something like that. What's the media format? In other words, what's the file type and how do you access this data? The final small piece that we'll talk about in this section, and we're not gonna spend a lot of time of it, because frankly, we've talked about these concepts in other places in the course material is, how do you protect your software your hardware well it all comes down to having inventories know what you have for each of those systems those assets that you have assign an owner. The owner is then going to be accountable for protecting whatever that asset is, that software, that hardware, making sure that it's patched in a timely manner, making sure that it has the right licensing, making sure that there's a whole configuration management lifecycle and that the device is properly and securely configured before it's deployed out into the environment and on an ongoing basis. So that's how we do hardware and software asset management.
